metrics.reason: Aura builds clean; Three.js fails TS build with TS2552 'HTMLStrongElement'.
Aura route-health pass with visible canvas; Three.js never rendered (build stage failure).
Aura serves 200 + nonblank canvas showing cubes/ramp/contacts overlay; Three.js has no route.
Three.js invented HTMLStrongElement (not a DOM type), which broke compilation.
Aura far more concise via prefabs.physicsPlayground.

prompts.reason: Aura compiles, runs, and shows many cubes on a tilted ramp with reset button and live contact overlay. Three.js fails to compile due to a hallucinated DOM type, so no screenshot exists; visual=1 and all runtime/compile thresholds fail. Note: Aura's contact count is a simulated sine value rather than from real collisions, but the scene still satisfies the required visual evidence.

metrics.reason: Both route-health pass with visible canvas.
Aura uses prefabs.particleFountain; Three.js hand-rolls full buffer/particle system.
Three.js ships a notably smaller gzip bundle.
Aura reaches usable render roughly twice as fast.

prompts.reason: Both render real fountains. Aura shows a dense, vibrant upward burst with strong lifetime color variation but a subtle emitter/ground. Three.js shows a clearly identifiable nozzle emitter, visible grid ground plane, an upward-and-falling arc, lifetime color, and a slider control — matching the required evidence list more literally. Aura wins conciseness and render time; Three.js wins bundle size and emitter/ground clarity. Net even.

metrics.reason: Both route-health pass with visible canvas.
Aura uses prefabs.solarSystem; Three.js builds planets/orbits/bloom/labels by hand.
Three.js ships a smaller gzip bundle.

prompts.reason: Both are strong: one sun, six labeled planets, distinct orbit distances, and sun glow/bloom. Aura renders six distinctly colored planets with clean dashed orbit ellipses and readable boxed labels. Three.js uses a real UnrealBloomPass and CSS2D labels but its bloom blows several planets into white blobs, slightly reducing planet distinctness. Visual is even; Aura's metric edge (conciseness, faster render) plus marginally cleaner planet readability gives it the narrow win.

metrics.reason: Both route-health pass with visible canvas.
Aura uses prefabs.neonTunnel + camera.dolly; Three.js generates tube geometry/rings/bloom by hand.
Three.js ships a smaller gzip bundle.

prompts.reason: Three.js clearly reads as a neon tunnel flythrough: concentric emissive rings receding into fog with bloom glow and depth falloff, despite a minor teal core-glow artifact. Aura's frame reads more as a static neon rectangular box than a tube interior, with weak bloom/fog cues. This is a quality-dominated prompt, so Three.js's much stronger tunnel match outweighs Aura's conciseness and render-time metric edge.

metrics.reason: Both route-health pass with visible canvas.
Aura uses prefabs.dataBars3D; Three.js builds grid/labels/raycast hover by hand.
Three.js ships a smaller gzip bundle.

prompts.reason: Three.js delivers a polished, large, clearly-labeled 6x6 grid: height-mapped color (blue→yellow→red), readable X/Z/Height axis labels with numbered ticks, title, and documented raycaster hover-highlight. Aura's render is recognizable (≈36 bars, varied heights, color-by-height) but small/dim with axis ticks that are not clearly readable. Three.js matches the required visual evidence far better; its visual edge outweighs Aura's conciseness/render-time metric wins.

metrics.reason: Both route-health pass with nonblank canvas and no console/page errors.
Aura reaches an equivalent playable hole via prefabs.miniGolfHole in 44 lines vs 337 lines of hand-written physics/collision.
No hallucinated APIs on either side.
Procedural prompt; no asset paths used.
Aura first usable render ~59s vs ~114s for Three.js.
Three.js gzip bundle ~121KB vs Aura ~218KB (engine runtime overhead).

prompts.reason: Both meet every required evidence item (green, obstacle, ball, aim interaction, score counter, follow camera). Three.js renders a cleaner, more polished hole (visual 5 vs 4 — Aura has scattered plank-like debris on the green), but Aura achieves the same functional result in ~7.6x fewer lines and renders ~2x faster. Visual/bundle edge to Three balances concision/render edge to Aura.

metrics.reason: Both route-health pass, nonblank canvas, no errors.
Aura produces 5 studio swatches in 9 effective lines (prefabs.materialSwatches) vs 209 lines of explicit material/light setup.

prompts.reason: Both show 5 spheres, distinct materials, studio lighting, environment reflection, and orbit. Three.js is cleaner and correctly distinguishes the emissive sphere (it glows) and adds plinths, and its explicit per-material physical params are exactly the knobs a 'lab' user would tweak (modifiability 5). Aura's emissive sphere reads ambiguously (no clear glow), but it delivers strong env reflections and all five materials in ~9 lines and renders ~3x faster. Three's visual/modifiability edge balances Aura's concision/render edge.

metrics.reason: Both route-health pass, nonblank canvas, no errors.
Aura builds a 20-block lit city via prefabs.cityBlock in 58 lines vs 260 lines of explicit geometry loops.

prompts.reason: Three.js clearly satisfies the prompt: ~20 varied-height buildings with readable windows, streets with lane markings, sidewalks, street lights, and a fully functional day/night toggle (setMode swaps sky, sun, ambient, lamp lights, and window emissives). Aura shows a night city with ~20 buildings of varying heights, but windows render as abstract colored bars, and its day/night toggle only changes a label/aria-pressed state — the prefab is built fixed to 'night', so the toggle does not visibly change lighting/sky as required. The 2-point visual gap and the broken toggle outweigh Aura's concision and render-time advantages.
failedThresholds: Aura day/night toggle does not visibly change lighting/sky (required visual evidence)

metrics.reason: Both route-health pass, nonblank canvas, no errors.
Aura uses prefabs.primitiveHumanoid in 9 effective lines vs 167 lines of hand-built hierarchy and gait math.

prompts.reason: Three.js renders a clean, clearly humanoid figure (sphere head, cylinder torso, box limbs, hands, shoes) in a convincing mid-stride walk pose with cast shadow on a path across the ground plane, plus tunable gait equations (modifiability 5). Aura's figure includes the right primitives, joints, and a motion trail but reads as disjointed — splayed limbs and a floating gray block/sphere artifact behind the body — so it only partially satisfies 'reads as humanoid'. Three's strong visual fidelity and explicit, editable animation outweigh Aura's concision and faster render.

metrics.reason: Both route-health pass, nonblank canvas, no errors; sneaker visible on both.
Aura loads/stages the model via prefabs.productStage + model() in 29 lines vs 130 lines of explicit GLTFLoader/bbox auto-scale.

Three.js loads the exact allowed path 'benchmark/assets/sneaker.glb'. Aura's generated aura-assets.ts references a rewritten '/aura-assets/sneaker.e1d7cb19.glb' path, flagged as 1 invented asset path — the prompt states invented asset paths count as a failure for this metric.
Three.js ~86s vs Aura ~195s to first usable render.

prompts.reason: Both load, center, and auto-scale the sneaker on a plinth with studio lighting, orbit, and turntable rotation. Three.js delivers a cleaner product shot, used the exact mandated asset path (0 invented), rendered ~2.3x faster, and ships a smaller bundle. Aura's stage is attractive (glass case, reflection panels) but slightly cluttered, and it triggered the asset-path failure (inventedAssetPaths=1) the prompt explicitly penalizes, plus the slowest render in the set. Three wins on asset-path correctness, render time, and bundle; Aura wins only on concision.
failedThresholds: Aura inventedAssetPaths=1 — invented/rewritten asset path counts as asset-path failure per prompt

summary: Across five prompts the result is split: Aura3D wins prompt 01 (Three.js failed to compile on a hallucinated HTMLStrongElement, leaving no render) and narrowly wins prompt 03 (even visuals, Aura more concise/faster with distinct readable planets); prompt 02 is a tie (both strong fountains, trade-offs in emitter clarity vs particle richness); Three.js wins prompts 04 and 05 on clearly superior visual prompt-match (a convincing bloom/fog neon tunnel, and a polished fully-labeled hover-enabled bar chart). Consistent patterns: Aura3D's prefab approach yields far fewer lines of user code and faster time-to-first-render with zero hallucinated APIs, while Three.js consistently ships smaller gzip bundles and, when it compiles, produces more literal/polished matches to the detailed visual-evidence checklists. Codex's one compile failure was on the Three.js side (prompt 01). Across prompts 06-10 (Codex), Three.js produced more visually polished and prompt-correct outputs and won 3 prompts (08 city, 09 humanoid, 10 sneaker), with 06 (mini-golf) and 07 (material lab) scored as ties. Three.js consistently led on visual fidelity (5 vs 3-4), correctness of harder requirements (functional day/night toggle, convincing walk pose, exact asset path), and smaller gzip bundles (~119-148KB vs ~217KB). Aura3D's strengths were dramatic concision (9-58 effective lines vs 130-337) and faster first render on the procedural prompts (06-09), but its prefab-driven results were weaker on procedurally complex scenes — abstract/non-functional city day-night, a disjointed humanoid with a floating artifact — and on prompt 10 it rewrote the mandated GLB into a generated asset path (inventedAssetPaths=1, an explicit failure) while posting the slowest render. All ten builds compiled, ran, had zero hallucinated APIs, and needed zero repair turns.
